card.intuition: A deque that stays sorted by evicting smaller (or larger) elements from the back and expired elements from the front — the front always holds the current window's maximum (or minimum).
card.analogy: A priority boarding line where a new VIP can push out all less-important passengers behind them, and passengers who've already boarded (left the window) are removed from the front.
card.time_complexity: O(n) total — each element enters and leaves the deque exactly once
card.space_complexity: O(k) where k is the window size
when_to_use: Sliding window maximum or minimum in O(n) — the core primitive
Jump game variants where you need the max/min reachable in the last k steps
Any DP recurrence of the form dp[i] = f(max/min of dp[i-k..i-1])
gotchas: Store indices in the deque, not values — you need indices to check expiry (front is out of window) and to write results
Evict from back when new element is >= current back (for max deque) — using > would keep duplicates; >= removes stale smaller elements immediately
Evict from front when front index is <= i - k (no longer in the window of size k)

exercise.prompt: Sliding window maximum using a monotonic deque. Return the max of each window of size k.
hints: Pop from the back (dq.pop()) when the new element is larger — those smaller elements can never be the window max
The current window maximum is always at the front of the deque: nums[dq[0]]
exercise.test_code:
assert sliding_window_max([1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7], 3) == [3,3,5,5,6,7]
assert sliding_window_max([1], 1)                   == [1]
assert sliding_window_max([1,2,3,4,5], 3)           == [3,4,5]
assert sliding_window_max([5,4,3,2,1], 3)           == [5,4,3]
assert sliding_window_max([1,3,1,2,0,5], 3)         == [3,3,2,5]
print('PASS')
